Having already played over 180 hours on Xbox, I highly recommend this game! It was a real pleasure to dive back into the games world on Steam Deck, and its even better thanks to the Steam Workshop. Of course, the game isnt perfect. The biggest flaw, in my opinion, is the character controls, which are sometimes really bad. This is especially true when the character is swimming. Thats why I missed out on a ton of points of interest on the Skellige Islands. I also didnt particularly like the horse controls, especially after playing Red Dead Redemption II. I also want to point out that there are often too many dialogue options. I would have preferred these choices to appear only when they actually impact the storyline. Despite that, its a great game that I highly recommend. If youre hesitating to buy it, go for it!

The combat system is good, I won't say great, I understand there isn't much to offer for sword fights since there are tons of different creatures but at least they should have gone with more interesting hand-to-hand combat style. 910 As with most people I tried starting this game 2-3 times since release over 10 years ago. With the hype of Witcher 4 I decided to lock in to this epic. Spent around 50 hours on here and 30 on PS5 to beat just the main story, which is all my review is... Game play is NOT dated as some may say, as I highly enjoyed the swordplay and signs. Geralt is a true badass when time slows down and the dismembering of monsters occurs. The game visually has aged well in my opinion. I played with no mods or enhancements. The scenery and foliage still look breath taking to me. The facial animation on the characters do show age though. Story wise, I mean its amazing. That is the only word I can label it with. It has every fantasy fan's cravings in it. From monsters to black smiths. Side quests are always a treat and Geralt's commentary makes it worth it with his smart ass but humble remarks. Truly a one of a kind character and I cant see where they take this next.

SO GOOD! I can't believe I didn't play this sooner. I played Cyberpunk 2077 right after I got my gaming PC, and thought I should check out CDPR's other titles. When I looked at Witcher 3 though, I didn't like the theme  style  genre as much, and skipped it. 4 years later and Witcher 3 was on sale for a few bucks. I thought I would give it a try and compare, and see why it had such a high metacritic score and so many people who call it one of the greatest. The hype was real! Even though I am not too into this type of game, I got SUPER into Witcher 3 and couldn't stop playing it. Beat all main  secondary quests in the base game, Hearts of Stone, and Blood and Wine. The combat is ok not great. Graphics are ok which is amazing considering it is 2026, 10 years after it was released. But the story and characters are soooo good. Really hits. Loved Geralt, Ciri, even stupid Roach, Yen, vamps, etc. The story is pretty linear with only a few ways to alter the ending. Lots to explore  do. There is a lot to learn at the start, and it is overwhelming, but worth it. I enjoyed gwent and got into it. Played and won all the tournaments highest score was 300. Horse riding and Roach were my main gripe. They were super annoying at times. Lots of fun mods out there that are worth checking out. They can really help tweak the game and make it a perfect fit for you. In the end, I think I liked this more than Cyberpunk 2077. Witcher 3 is a 1010 masterpiece.

Strong Suits Main Story 1010 The games strongest point is definitely the story. It's about 50 hours long for the Base Game, 6-8 hours long in Hearts of Stone, and 10 hours long in Blood and Wine. The Villains are definitely interesting, with the strong points being Geralt and the Side Characters, as well as the many options the game gives you. From what I can assume, there is like 50 different ways the story can go. Definitely one of the best stories I've played. Side Content 1010 Another one of the game's best areas, is the side content. Most of the game's characters have associated side quests, which helps develop the character and can also influence the main story and the world around you. I've played only a few games with side content that actually matters, or influences anything, and this is one of them. Open World 1210 Probably the best Open-World, or one of the best Open-Worlds out there. Each area is unique and different, with even Blood  Wine adding an entire new area, and I'm pretty sure one of the most sizeable ones. It's definitely easy to get lost in certain parts of the map and explore, and the 3 Cities Novigrad, Oxenfurt, and Beauclair are all very dense, but well crafted. Visuals  Atmosphere 1010 The Visuals in this game are something else for being made in 2015. The Graphics are amazing, and you can quite easily run 120 FPS if you have a decent-good PC. Each and every area looks and feels different, with the Sound Track  SFX being amazing. Definitely one of the most visually astonishing games on the market, especially for when it was made. Minor Flaws Quality of Life 710 The game has a lot of great quality of life features, but there are something issues I do have with the games quality of life, mostly during the later game. I enjoy the games fast travel system, and I also love the fact that you can store loot and items you find around the world in chests. My main issue with the games quality of life though lies after you beat the game. Once you finish the game, almost ALL of the games main side characters completely disappear, no dialogues, no shops, no nothing, and in my opinion, that is kind of a bummer, since I wish I could go back and talk to some of the side characters after beating the main story, this also applies to both DLCs, with the only talkable character in Blood and Wine being Anna, if you do get the good ending. Combat 610 The Combat is probably the weakest part of the Witcher 3, at least in my opinion. For context, I played this game on KBM Keyboard  Mouse and not Controller, so it could differ between the two. The main weakness of the combat for me was functionality. It was incredibly clunky, and the animations were weird, as well as fighting multiple enemies being clunky. The base combat is also overly basic, it's just dodge hit dodge hit dodge hit, over and over again, in a very basic manner. The strengths of the combat though come from the Alchemy portion. The Alchemy in this game is amazing, there's almost a hundred potions if not more, with each and every one serving a purpose, and allowing you to give yourself advantages against certain enemy types. The spells in this game are also unique, allowing you to utilize them for different purposes. So all in all, it's not the worst combat system, but it definitely could use improvements. Bosses 6.310 This is probably the hardest one for me to score, since some of the Boss Fights are great, and some aren't so great. The early-mid game boss fights are pretty decent, but quite boring, but the late game boss fights are not great. A lot of the Wild Hunt boss fights are incredibly repetitive, they spam the same 3 attacks over and over again, and are quite easy. I simply just wish they had more variety in their attacks, or weren't so annoying to fight, but nonetheless, they are fairly easy and should take less than 5 tries. The final boss fight was okay ish, he was very tanky so it took awhile to kill him, but he was definitely the most fun. With the DLCs, Blood  Wine by far has the best boss fights. I enjoyed almost all the boss fights in that DLC, with most of them having the most engaging move sets, while having great lore attached to them. Hearts of Stone though, not so much. Most of the boss fights were incredibly annoying, usually either having an ability that allowed them to heal themselves, or create like 20 clones of themselves. Nonetheless, this game has some bad boss fights, but also some great boss fights. Overall Score 9.310

The Witcher 3 is the best role playing game i have ever had the pleasure of playing. Yes it frequently crashes on PC and yes the combat does feel a little dated by todays standards however, there is no doubting that this game is truly something special and you will quickly forgive its quirks for the amazing and concentrated story. It immediately hooks you in and kept me invested throughout my 100 hours playthrough. The characters are well thought out and each have a unique role. The worlds to explore are abundant and packed with optional side quests, treasure hunts, Witcher contracts and places to explore. The music is also excellent and matches the tone of the game very well, perfectly fitting with the current situation you are in. There is a lot to learn in The Witcher 3 but mastering even just the basics will reward you in your journey. The gameplay including combat is showing its age but is still perfectly acceptable and fun. I especially enjoyed playing through the Blood and Wine DLC with the vibrant and rich world of Toussaint drawing me deeper into the world of The Witcher 3. I highly recommend playing the Witcher 3 even in 2026.

Pros Story - S Tier Characters - S tier Quests - S tier  World building and Huge map are also great.  choice-based dialogue options  gwent is a very fun card game . collecting the cards and the amount of heroes to choose from is also a plus. Cons Loot - There is a lot of it, but not a lot of variety. Mostly just swords or the occasional axe but those are really the only 2 options of melee combat. Most of the loot you are gonna find is gonna be worthless or you are going to out-level it. So yes lots of loot to find, but no not a lot of it to actually use and the variety is lacking. Swords arent really unique they will just have slightly better stats.. so getting a new legendary sword isnt really exciting. ItemsEquipment - The crafting, alchemy, potions, bombs, range combat equipment are all very lacking. I played much of the game without really need to find ingredients, make potions, find and use runestones, upgrade your bombs etc. There are tons of potions but they dont really do enough to feel needed. Yes you could play on a harder difficulty but still bombs and potions arent NEARLY as important as just getting good at timing parries and dodges. They didnt really do a good job at making all of this extra stuff in the game feel needed. also i have a huge amount of money with like nothing to spend it on. traversal - Traversing map the world sucksss. your horse will not move if you touch any tiny tree or bush, so traveling on horseback is often a pain of roach halting every 10 seconds. Your horse and Geralt will not go up any incline that is like more than 30 degrees. its hard to tell what hills you can go over or not. and with tons of large mountains in much of the map that you cant traverse. getting around is often more of a chore than enjoyment. Sliding down tiny hills, mantiling, geralt's jumping are all very janky and very much a pain. you cannot fast travel unless you are at specific sign posts making the awful traversing on horseback a requirement. all in all traversing the world is comically bad in this otherwise great game. combatgameplay - likely the most dissapointing aspect of the witcher. The combat is very boring. To start crossbows and bombs range combat are very weak and they are also auto-targeting , which feels pretty dissapointing to use. The melee combat is very boring. its just dodge  spam attack , dodge  spam attack. its a very boring and un-engaging combat loop. if not for the other aspects of the witcher being S-tier, witcher would be a very mid RGP with how boring the combat is... but you just cant knock the other things that witcher just nails. theres ton of quests in this game. and tons of the quests are fun and not just tedious side-quests most rpgs fall into. The characters are great. the story is great. The choice based aspect is also a highlight, you get to choose how you approach situations and which dialogue options which is always a huge plus in RPGs.

The Witcher 3 is one of those rare games that completely pulls you into its world. The story is emotional and mature, the side quests are better written than most main stories in other games, and every choice feels like it actually matters. Combat blends swordplay, magic, and alchemy, and while it can feel clunky at first, it becomes rewarding once you learn the systems. The soundtrack and atmosphere elevate the experience even more. Comes with 2 DLCs, which to call them that would be an insult, since I'd call them almost stand-alone games, capable of captivating you on their own. It delivers one of the most engaging and emotional RPG experiences available, making it a must-play for fantasy fans.

I'm on my 4th playthrough, and every time I reach Skellige and meet with Yennefer during King Bran's funeral, I start feeling emo. I don't know if it's the musical score mixed with the choices I make during dialogue, but I just love how honest everything between Yennefer and Geralt feels. This is by far the best game I've ever played. Everything about it is perfect, and it makes me feel like I'm completely part of it. I'm extremely attached to these extraordinarily well-written characters. The world and its people are depicted as something very flawed. I respect how the game shows that there can still be happiness amidst all the misery and that even a lot of the choices exist in a morally gray sense, which feels realistic, and it's something that is not portrayed in any other game to this extent. Masterful journey through and through. Hearts of Stone is the epitome of storytelling, and Blood and Wine is incredible as it plays out as a literal new game. The combat is very intricate, and you'll need to read about all the mechanics to actually play the game how you're supposed to instead of spamming light attacks with QUEN like most people do. You'll be rewarded if you apply the right oils, drink the right potions, and use the right builds depending on the sets you craft, if you do. I love the art style and how recognizable the RED Engine also looks. The soundtrack is incredible. The Wolven Storm, After The Storm, Kaer Morhen, The Vagabond, Blood and Wine, On Thin Ice, The Hunt is Coming, For Toussaint!, Ard Skellig Village and Hearts of Stone are some of the few tracks that instantly come into my mind when I think of The Witcher. This is, by far, the best game ever made, and no other has expansions as good as this one has. The bar is extremely high, and it will be very hard to top it.

Ive completed The Witcher 3 Wild Hunt three times, and its still one of my favorite games everand easily my favorite in the Witcher series. What makes it special for me is the atmosphere. The world feels deeply rooted in Slavic folklore, and that gives it a unique, almost authentic soul. The music is powerful and emotional, inspired by traditional folk sounds, that make every moment feel alive. The quests deserve a special mention. Even simple side quests can turn into unexpected moral dilemmas. Sometimes theres no good choice, only consequencesand thats what makes it so memorable. Also, Geralt and Yennefer? One of my favorite love stories in any game. Its not perfect or sweetits complicated, intense, and feels real. The chemistry, the arguments, the history between themyou can feel that these two have been through a lot together. Yes, I always choose Yennefer. No regrets. Even after three playthroughs, I still discover new details, new dialogues, new outcomes. This game doesnt just tell you a storyit pulls you into its world and doesnt let go.

Over 260 hours on record, and I still get the urge to reinstall it just to play a few hands of Gwent. Lets be real, The Witcher 3 doesn't just raise the bar for RPGs it completely breaks it and ruins other games for you. The most insane thing about this game is the writing. Youll pick up a random, seemingly boring side quest to find an old lady's missing frying pan, and somehow it spirals into a deeply emotional story involving espionage and war crimes. The world feels incredibly lived-in, dark, and unforgiving. Your choices actually matter and carry a heavy weight, often forcing you to choose between the lesser of two evils where nobody really gets a happy ending. And then there's the immersion. Riding through Velen while the trees sway in the wind, with that hauntingly beautiful soundtrack playing in the background, is an experience you don't forget. And the DLCs? Hearts of Stone has one of the best villains in gaming history, and Blood and Wine is basically an entire sequel disguised as an expansion. Is it 100 flawless? Nah. The combat can feel a bit floaty and repetitive once you figure out your build, swimming controls like you're steering a broken fridge, and Roach will absolutely spawn on a roof or get stuck on a tiny fence when you need her most. But honestly, none of that matters. The sheer scale, the storytelling, and the atmosphere make those minor annoyances irrelevant. If you haven't played this yet, do yourself a favor buy it, get the expansions, say goodbye to your social life for the next couple of months, and remember... always accept a match of Gwent.
If you don't have this in your library, youre doing gaming wrong. Its the gold standard. Period.

h1Beautiful World, Frustrating Experienceh1 I wanted to enjoy The Witcher 3 for its story and world, hoping to escape from the stress and danger of real life. At first, the game felt calm and immersive, like a safe place to explore and experience a story. But soon, it felt like the game betrayed me. It masks itself as an interesting story with a big open world, and in some ways, it deliversbut in reality, it feels more like Dark Souls. Difficult fights, punishing mechanics, and tedious resource management overshadow the story, and that is what I hate most in video games. Even when I tried to play story-only, I was forced into challenging battles, crafting, and mechanics that distract from the narrative. Gathering ingredients for potions and bombs is tedious and poorly guided, making preparation a chore rather than fun. While the graphics and music are stunning, the Slavic dark-fantasy setting and the adult scene at the beginning arent flaws of the gamethey just personally make it hard for me to fully enjoy. Some parts felt designed to shock rather than immerse, which distracted from the story I wanted to experience. Overall, the game has moments of beauty and depth, but for me, the mechanics and certain narrative choices made it stressful rather than enjoyable. If you primarily care about story and immersion without combat or explicit content, The Witcher 3 might not meet your expectations.

Fantasy as a genre is something I find myself drawn to a lot of the time but it's also very hard to get right. Just having a world of swords, shields, magic, elves, and dragons isn't enough for you to get it right. More often than not it leaves me experiences a lot of fantasy worlds but never truly falling in love with them. There is just so much that Witcher 3 gets right that I don't even know if you can list everything. However, the actual world itself in my opinion is what sells this universe. The environments feel endless and all of these areas are filled to the brim with monsters and magical oddities that fit the gritty vibe the Witcher is known for. Cities are densely packed and feel very real unlike most other games where the cities feel like a doll house. At this point I think most people know that the story is incredible. The way that your choices genuinely change character storylines and the world as a whole makes you feel apart of it. You get to see nuanced characters deal with this world that is at war with a complexity and realism that builds up the world and story. The combat itself isn't too impressive which is fine to me. If anything the combat gets more engaging the higher difficulty you choose because it forces you to actually delve deep into mechanics that you will never need to on the easiest difficulties. If you're willing to look past that it is a must-play for anyone who enjoys RPGs.

Re-playing on a higher spec PC after a few years, with the free update for the next gen visuals, the game looks stunning and the story is a compelling and fun as ever. Overall- Story- 7.510- voice acting is top notch and characters are unique throughout. Some side quests do become repetitive and there are some similarities between them that could have been lightly altered to add to the immersion a bit. The main story quests are mainly well structured and do a good job of introducing new features and mechanics, but some of the grind becomes annoying at harder difficulties. I played at the second hardest difficulty, which I feel made it a bit harder to focus on the story, as I was constantly crafting and optimizing weapons for encounters rather than 100 focusing on the story. Had I played at a lower difficulty, I can see myself giving a higher rating for the story. Combat - 710 - Some of the lower aspect of the score might be a result of having played more recent games that do some of the combat aspects more justice. For example, the sword play is fun and there is a large range of different mechanics and crafting materials you can use to enhance your weapons and defenses, but using them in combat is a bit clunky at times, and overall many of the enemy moves and attacks become easily judged after some time. Compared to a game like Elden Ring or DS games for example, the harder enemies and bosses are not nearly as challenging. They definitely pose a challenge at harder game difficulties, but their moves and use of the environment is a bit limited for such an open world game. Additionally, mounted combat leaves a lot to be desired and feels particularly unpolished compared to other games with mounted combat, like Elden Ring, Crimson Desert, or even RDR2 even though that combat is usually ranged. Overall, the horse movement across the world does break immersion a bit, and 99 of the time I dismount to fight enemies if they are mounted, since their movement is predictable and slow. Sword combat is fun and feels fresh across the first playthrough. I wish it was a bit harder to lightheavy attack spam most enemies, and the i-frames around finishing moves back it pretty easy to string kills together. This makes the game fun and cinematic at times, which is great  think like original Assassin's Creed games like II and Revelations, where chaining kills becomes easy as you level up, but also makes fighting most enemies that litter the environment more of a nuisance at times than an actual challenge. WorldEnvironment - 910 - Easily one of the better constructed open world games I have played. There are very few times where the world feels empty or unpopulated. On Ultra details, there is simply never a dull area, be it a battlefield littered with bodies, weapons, and rotting animals, or a small fishing village with shops, smiths, and homes. The world feels alive, NPCs react to your character and your behavior in a pretty realistic way for the time this game came out, and stumbling upon side quests or civilians in distress to rescue actually feels organic and derived from exploration, not just following pings or map markers. GraphicsPerformanceBugs - 810 - The game looks stunning for a 10 year old title. On my system, the game runs flawlessly at 1440p, FPS never dips to a point where it is at all noticeable to me, on all settings maxed out other than not running 4K resolution. There is definitely some noticeable pop-in at long distances, and some terrain fill and meshing becomes a little visible if you rapidly sweep the camera or enter a new area, but it's never really immersion breaking. As for bugs, I would say it was a bit disappointing to still encounter map geometry breakage, T-posing character at times, and buggy waterswimming interactions during one early mission in a cave that led to having to reload the game from a prior save to reset. For a game that has received this much support and updates, given I encountered many of these bugs in the first 10 hours of the game, I would hope they would have been addressed by now. However, they are more of an annoyance than anything else, and won't affect 99 of your actual playthrough in my experience. Overall - 810 - Given the game is frequently on sale and already discounted, I think this is a no brainer. Anyone looking to jump into The Witcher 4 soon who has not played 3, should definitely give it a playthrough if you can.

The Witcher 3 Wild Hunt - A Timeless Journey Through a Living World Even after completing the game multiple times, including New Game Plus runs, The Witcher 3 Wild Hunt continues to stand as one of the most immersive RPG experiences ever created. Story - 8.510 The narrative is rich, layered, and emotionally engaging. It doesnt just tell a story, it pulls you into a morally complex world where choices matter, often without clear right or wrong answers. While not flawless, its depth and character development make it unforgettable. Graphics - 710 Original  8.510 Next-Gen Update For its original release, the visuals were impressive, though not groundbreaking by todays standards. However, the next-gen update breathes new life into the game, significantly enhancing lighting, textures, and overall atmosphere, bringing it closer to modern visual expectations. Combat - Accessible but Less Challenging Compared to Souls-like games, the combat is more forgiving and approachable. It lacks the punishing difficulty some players seek, but it compensates with fluid mechanics, variety in builds, and the satisfying use of signs, potions, and swordplay. Exploration  World Design - Exceptional This is where the game truly shines. The world is vast, detailed, and alive. Every region feels distinct, filled with meaningful side quests, hidden stories, and environmental storytelling. Its not just big, its purposeful. Roach Mechanics - A Minor Flaw Traversal is generally smooth, but controlling Roach can sometimes feel clunky, especially when compared to more refined mounts like Torrent Elden Ring. Its a small issue, but noticeable during exploration. Comparison to Previous Titles The Witcher 3 offers significantly more depth, scale, and freedom compared to its predecessor. It expands on everything - storytelling, world-building, and gameplay systems, making it a definitive upgrade. Final Verdict - 810 A vast, beautiful, and deeply engaging world that continues to hold its ground years after release. While it has minor flaws, the overall experience is something few games have matched. A game you dont just play, but you live in. And this World will live with me forever.

I play this game in other plataform and i bought this in steam. Playing this in 2026 Ive always struggled to find the right words to describe how I feel while playing, but The Witcher 3 deserves the effort. This isnt just another open-world game, its an experience that completely absorbs you. What impressed me the most was the richness of the dialogue. Unlike other games where you just skip through conversations, here I wanted to hear every word. Each interaction feels real, and while the missions follow a linear structure that keeps you focused, they are so well written that even the smallest task feels important to the world. The level of detail is insane. You can lose track of time just taking in the scenery or watching how the villages react to the weather and the monsters. The world feels alive, organic, and dangerous. The soundtrack is perfect, without exaggeration, its one of the best Ive ever heard. The music sets the tone for every moment, from the tension of an epic combat to the melancholy of exploring an isolated forest. You can't play this without the music. This is the kind of game where you jump in just for half an hour and suddenly five hours have passed. The way the story hooks you and how your decisions shape the fate of the characters is something few games manage to do this well. If youre looking for a game with soul, tons of content, and a story that stays with you long after the credits roll, dont think twice. Its worth every cent and every hour invested. b1010 - WHAT A MASTER PIECE!b

Masterpiece  Score 1010 If your soul is tired of endlessly swiping through shallow profiles,  The Witcher 3 is the long-term relationship youve been looking for. Its a massive, beautiful world where every match has about fifty hours of emotional baggage and a side quest that will probably make you cry.  The core gameplay is the ultimate romantic fantasy you play as a professional fixer who looks great in leather and spends his time tracking down a lost loved one while getting distracted by literally everyone else's problems. Its a loop of ride a horse, talk to a ghost, play a card game for three hours, and occasionally fight a griffin.  Its the only game where you can be in the middle of a world-ending crisis and still stop to ask a merchant for a round of Gwentwhich is honestly how most of us handle our real-life problems anyway. A must-play for anyone who wants a dating experience where the monsters are literal, the choices actually matter, and your horse is the only one who truly understands you.  Play this if you enjoy Having a type, and that type is specifically silver-haired mutants  Spending more time on your card game addiction than the actual plot  Realizing that choosing the lesser evil is basically every Friday night out  A story so good it makes your real-life social circle look like a bunch of NPCs

I cant say enough good things about . This game completely pulled me innot just because of the main story, but because of how rich and alive the entire world feels. What really stood out to me were the stories and side quests. Theyre not fillerthey feel meaningful, emotional, and often hit harder than the main plot in other games. Every contract, every village, every character has depth, and you actually care about the outcomes of your choices. The monster lore is on another level. I especially loved how the game leans into dark folkloretwisted, unsettling, and rooted in real-world legends. The way creatures like witches are portrayedterrifying, predatory, and deeply disturbingadds a layer of horror that makes the world feel dangerous and authentic. Its not just fantasy it feels like stepping into the darker corners of old myths and stories. That horror element is what elevates the experience for me. The game doesnt shy away from the grim and eerie aspects of folklore, and it executes them in a way thats both chilling and fascinating. Youre not just fighting monstersyoure uncovering the tragic, often horrifying stories behind them. Overall, this is one of the most immersive and unforgettable games Ive ever played. Incredible storytelling, rich lore, and a hauntingly beautiful worldThe Witcher 3 sets a standard that few games even come close to.

The Witcher 3 is, without exaggeration, one of the greatest games ever made. There is not much left to say that has not already been said, yet finishing it still leaves a feeling that is difficult to describe. At first, not every part of its story reveals its full weight. But as you move forward, piece by piece, everything grows deeper, richer, and more meaningful, until the game becomes something far greater than just a journey  it becomes a world you truly live in. What stays with me the most is the emptiness after the ending. Standing in Corvo Bianco, beside Triss, looking into the distance while the beautiful music of Toussaint plays, it is hard to believe that the story is over. There is a strange silence in that moment  not a bad one, but the kind that comes after something unforgettable. Few games leave behind this feeling. Fewer still make you feel as though you have not simply finished a game, but lived through the ending of a long and meaningful chapter. That is why, for me, The Witcher 3 is not just a masterpiece  it is the best game in the world.

TW3, along with its expansions, has been the greatest gaming experience I've had so far it is incredibly rich and definitely deserves more than one, two, or even three playthroughs. The amount of content, and its quality, is absurd, without even considering both expansions, which greatly expand the mechanics and overall experience of the game. Now that I've fully completed both, the main story and most of the side content, I plan on reading the books and then playing TW1 and TW2 before starting a new playthrough NG of this masterpiece.
Once you get into the world of witcher 3 and its story, there's no turning back.

For me, this game is without a doubt number one. And Im certain it will remain so. A Polish studio of incredibly talented developers created a masterpiece that is forever written into the history of gaming. Playing this game was part of a larger journey for me - I read all 9 of Andrzej Sapkowskis books in the original language, and played through the entire trilogy in Polish as well. The Witcher 3 Wild Hunt became the perfect closing chapter of this unforgettable experience, ending it on an emotional high. Everything about this game feels exceptional to me - the unmatched atmosphere and music, the characters and their personal stories. You can truly feel how much passion and love went into creating it. Im not someone who tends to analyze games from a purely technical standpoint - mechanics, systems, and so on - although in my opinion, those aspects are also well done here. For me, games are more about the experience you live through, something that stays with you long after you finish it. And in that sense, The Witcher 3 reached deeper into my soul than any other game Ive ever played. Im really looking forward to The Witcher 4 and new adventures - this time with Ciri. But I truly hope well also get to see the beloved Geralt of Rivia again.
